title: How Many Lives Does God Take in the Bible: An Investigation into a Surprisingly High Body Count
description:
in Religion |   	March 31st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Whether or not we believe in any god, most of us here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry have the
body:
in Religion |   	March 31st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Whether or not we believe in any god, most of us here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry have the impresôÙsion of divine rulers overôÙlookôÙing humanôÙiôÙty with at least theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcal love and benevôÙoôÙlence. They forôÙgive us, they have plans for us, they nevôÙer close a door withôÙout openôÙing a winôÙdow, and so on. But in the parôÙticôÙuôÙlar case of the ChrisôÙtôÙian God, weãve all heard that he both giveth and taketh away, even if weãve nevôÙer so much as opened the Bible, Old TesôÙtaôÙment or New. That line comes from the Book of Job, which belongs to the Old, a text whose depicôÙtion of God may surôÙprise first-time readôÙers ã espeôÙcialôÙly in his willôÙingôÙness to cause death, the subôÙject of the HochelaôÙga video above on ãGodãs BibôÙliôÙcal Kill Count.ã
It turns out that, if you go through the King James VerôÙsion and talôÙly up every sinôÙgle perôÙson God kills on a spreadôÙsheet (a task to which HochelaôÙga creôÙator TomôÙmie Trelawny is sureôÙly among the best-suitôÙed YouTuôÙbers), you end up with a high numôÙber at the botôÙtom indeed. ãThroughôÙout the Old TesôÙtaôÙment, God is responôÙsiôÙble for a whole slew of natôÙurôÙal disôÙasôÙters,ã he says, ãfrom erasôÙing life on Earth in a world-endôÙing flood to unleashôÙing devôÙasôÙtatôÙing plagues ofã ã yes ã ãBibôÙliôÙcal proôÙporôÙtions.ã
ConôÙcerned as it is with layôÙing out Godãs law, the Old TesôÙtaôÙment, or Hebrew Bible, spends a great deal of time explainôÙing what hapôÙpens to the vioôÙlaôÙtors of those laws. In one pasôÙsage, 50,070 men are ãpulôÙverôÙized for glimpsôÙing inside the Ark of the Covenant,ã and, in anothôÙer, God sends an angel to ãwipe out 185,000 solôÙdiers in one night,ã to name just two inciôÙdents.
Trelawnyãs iniôÙtial count of the deaths the Bible attribôÙutôÙes to God comes to a preôÙcise-soundôÙing 2,559,449. But that figôÙure only includes instances in which the text specôÙiôÙfies how many peoôÙple died. SomeôÙtimes it doesôÙnãt, which requires the conôÙsciôÙenôÙtious bibôÙliôÙcal body-counter to rely on the best hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal estiôÙmates of, for examôÙple, how many peoôÙple an army or a city ã entiôÙties the Old TesôÙtaôÙment God could anniôÙhiôÙlate with a flick of the wrist ã comôÙprised at the time, to say nothôÙing of the EarthôÙãs total popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion at the preôÙsumpôÙtive time of the Flood. Trelawny goes with 20 milôÙlion, bringôÙing the final count to 24,681,116, about the same as the entire popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of ShangôÙhai. It may seem ironôÙic to draw a comôÙparôÙiôÙson with a city outôÙside what we could call the Judeo-ChrisôÙtôÙian world, but ChiôÙnese civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion has strict deities of its own. Run afoul of Leigong, for instance, and you could find yourôÙself struck down by a bolt of lightôÙning. But heãd sureôÙly have to get busy throwôÙing a whole lot more of them before even hopôÙing to approach the Lordãs record.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

The OriôÙgins of Satan: The EvoôÙluôÙtion of the DevôÙil in ReliôÙgion

74 Ways CharôÙacôÙters Die in Shakespeareãs Plays Shown in a Handy InfoôÙgraphôÙic: From Snakebites to Lack of Sleep

title: How Kraftwerkãs 22-Minute Song ãAutobahnã Became an Early Masterpiece in Electronic Music (1975)
description:
in History, Music, Technology |   	March 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It takes about five hours to driôÙve from Dû¥sôÙselôÙdorf to HamôÙburg on
body:
in History, Music, Technology |   	March 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It takes about five hours to driôÙve from Dû¥sôÙselôÙdorf to HamôÙburg on the AutoôÙbahn. DurôÙing that stretch, you can lisôÙten to Kraftwerkãs album AutoôÙbahn sevôÙen times ã or if you preôÙfer, you can loop its eponyôÙmous openôÙing song thirôÙteen times. For it was ãAutoôÙbahn,ãô more so than AutoôÙbahn, that changed the sound of music around the world in ways we still hear today. ãGerôÙmany was sudôÙdenôÙly on the musiôÙcal map,ã writes theô Guardianãs Tim Jonze. ãDavid Bowie ã who used to ride the autoôÙbahn while lisôÙtenôÙing to the record ã moved to Berlin and went on to make the elecôÙtronôÙiôÙcalôÙly influôÙenced Low, ãHeroesã and Lodger. BriôÙan Eno reloôÙcatôÙed to the rurôÙal vilôÙlage of Forst to record with the influôÙenôÙtial avant-garde band HarôÙmoôÙnia.ã Soon would come the elecôÙtronôÙic pop of UltraôÙvox, DAF and the EuryôÙthôÙmics, folôÙlowed byô DonôÙna SumôÙmer and GiorôÙgio Moroderãs floodôÙgate-openôÙing ãI Feel Loveã.
Not a bad pop-culôÙturôÙal coup for, as Jonze puts it, ãa 22-minute 43-secôÙond song about the GerôÙman road netôÙwork.ã At the time of its release in earôÙly 1975, Kraftwerk had put out three full albums, but what would become their sigôÙnaôÙture TeuôÙtonôÙic-elecôÙtronôÙic sound hadôÙnãt quite takôÙen shape. But it was already clear that their work took its inspiôÙraôÙtion from twenôÙtiôÙeth-cenôÙtuôÙry moderôÙniôÙty, a subôÙject of which no sinôÙgle work of man in their homeôÙland could have been more evocaôÙtive than the AutoôÙbahn.
With its oriôÙgins in the Weimar RepubôÙlic and its long stretchôÙes withôÙout a speed limôÙit, the GerôÙman freeôÙway netôÙwork is interôÙnaôÙtionôÙalôÙly regardôÙed as a conôÙcrete symôÙbol of total perôÙsonôÙal freeôÙdom, and total perôÙsonôÙal responôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty, withôÙin a highôÙly rule-respectôÙing culôÙture. To the young memôÙbers of Kraftwerk, who often drove the Dû¥sôÙselôÙdorf-HamôÙburg secôÙtion, it held out the promise of freeôÙdom.


	

So did the then-newô MinôÙiôÙmoog synôÙtheôÙsizôÙer, which cost as much as a VolkôÙswaôÙgen at the time, but offered the chance to make music like nothôÙing the pubôÙlic had ever heard before. ãAutoôÙbahnã capôÙtured the imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtions of lisôÙtenôÙers everyôÙwhere with not just its elecôÙtronôÙic effects, but also the inconôÙgruity of their comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion with instruôÙments like the flute (a holdover from Kraftwerkãs earôÙliôÙer comôÙpoôÙsiôÙtions) and vehicôÙuôÙlar sounds evocaôÙtive of a genôÙuine road trip ã all assemôÙbled at what would then have seemed a hypôÙnotôÙiôÙcalôÙly expanôÙsive length for a pop song. LitôÙtle did even the hippest lisôÙtenôÙers of the mid-sevôÙenôÙties, such as the AmerôÙiôÙcans tuned into earôÙly free-form FM staôÙtions where no corôÙpoôÙrate proôÙgramôÙming rules applied, know that they were hearôÙing what Jones calls ãthe point where elecôÙtronôÙic pop music truôÙly began.ã All car trips run out of road evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly, but humanôÙiôÙtyãs jourôÙney into the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙties of high-tech music shows no signs of approachôÙing its end.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The PsyôÙcheôÙdelôÙic AniôÙmatôÙed Video for Kraftwerkãs ãAutoôÙbahnã (1979)
Kraftwerk Plays a Live 40-Minute VerôÙsion of their SigôÙnaôÙture Song ãAutoôÙbahn:ã A SoundôÙtrack for a Long Road Trip (1974)
How Kraftwerk Made the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame
Kraftwerkãs First ConôÙcert: The BeginôÙning of the EndôÙlessôÙly InfluôÙenôÙtial Band (1970)

Hear the EvoôÙluôÙtion of ElecôÙtronôÙic Music: A SonôÙic JourôÙney from 1929 to 2019

title: Watch Errol Morrisãs Tune Out the Noise Free Online: A Documentary About the Financial Revolution That Transformed Investing
description: You canãt beat the marôÙket. That, at least, is the advice we all encounter earôÙly on when first we try our hand at investôÙing. HomeôÙspun thou
body:
You canãt beat the marôÙket. That, at least, is the advice we all encounter earôÙly on when first we try our hand at investôÙing. HomeôÙspun though it may sound, the idea has acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic roots: the EffiôÙcient MarôÙket HypothôÙeôÙsis, as the econôÙoôÙmists call it, holds that the prices in any finanôÙcial marôÙket already reflect all availôÙable inforôÙmaôÙtion relôÙeôÙvant to whatãs being tradôÙed withôÙin them. In the case of the stock marôÙket, for examôÙple, everyôÙthing known ã or indeed, knowôÙable ã about the future prospects of a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar comôÙpaôÙny is already incorôÙpoôÙratôÙed into its stock price, or might as well be. If the EMH is true, then it must also be true that nobody can beat the marôÙket, no matôÙter how deep their expeôÙriôÙence or develôÙoped their instinct for pickôÙing stocks.
Nobel LauôÙreôÙate econôÙoôÙmist Eugene Fama, whoãs done more than anyôÙone alive to refine the EFM and keep it in cirôÙcuôÙlaôÙtion, appears as one of the interôÙvieôÙwees inô Tune Out the Noise, the Errol MorôÙris-directôÙed docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary above. So do a range of othôÙer figôÙures, mostôÙly sepôÙtuôÙaôÙgeôÙnarôÙiôÙan and octoôÙgeôÙnarôÙiôÙan, whose great sucôÙcess in their fields owes to their havôÙing trustôÙed the wisôÙdom of the marôÙket. All have been involved with the investôÙment firm DimenôÙsionôÙal Fund AdviôÙsors, which, since its foundôÙing in the earôÙly nineôÙteen-eightôÙies, has been one of the engines of change in its indusôÙtry. In the first half of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry, investôÙing had an almost mysôÙtiôÙcal qualôÙiôÙty about it ã a qualôÙiôÙty swept away by the ãdata revôÙoôÙluôÙtionã of the secôÙond half.
That revôÙoôÙluôÙtion was powôÙered, of course, by comôÙputôÙers. Most of MorôÙrisã interôÙvieôÙwees first found themôÙselves placed in front of one of those hulkôÙing, inscrutable machines at some point in their terôÙtiary eduôÙcaôÙtion, more than likeôÙly at the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of ChicaôÙgo. They learned to work those earôÙly comôÙputôÙersã punch cards and whirring reels of tape even as elecôÙtronôÙic comôÙputôÙing itself first found its uses in civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion. SudôÙdenôÙly, though it demandôÙed painstakôÙing colôÙlecôÙtion and proôÙgramôÙming work, it had become posôÙsiôÙble to examôÙine stock marôÙket data and deterôÙmine what patôÙterns, if any, it conôÙtained, and whether any investor had conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly outôÙperôÙformed the averôÙage. The answers revealed would become the premise of not just ãpasôÙsiveã investôÙment firms like DFA, but also of the origôÙiôÙnal creôÙation of index funds like the S&P 500.
All this may not sound like the usuôÙal terôÙrain of Errol MorôÙris, whose preôÙviôÙous docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries have proôÙfiled everyôÙone from pet cemeôÙtery operôÙaôÙtors to forôÙmer U.S. secôÙreôÙtaries of defense to Stephen HawkôÙing. His films arenãt withôÙout their conôÙfrontaôÙtionôÙal moments, though givôÙen that Tune Out the Noise was comôÙmisôÙsioned by DFA itself, it shouldôÙnãt come as a surôÙprise that MorôÙris nevôÙer shifts into interôÙroôÙgaôÙtion mode (despite using his sigôÙnaôÙture InterôÙroôÙtron rig to shoot the interôÙviews). Despite claimôÙing not to know anyôÙthing about investôÙing or finanôÙcial marôÙkets going in, he finds plenôÙty of overôÙlap with interôÙests that have long run through his work: episôÙteôÙmolôÙoôÙgy, for examôÙple, and the nature of sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic revôÙoôÙluôÙtion. After all, most any field has some conôÙnecôÙtion to theô inexôÙhaustible subôÙject of how we know, what we know, and what we canãt know. ãPeoôÙple shrink from uncerôÙtainôÙty, but itãs uncerôÙtainôÙty that realôÙly creôÙates opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty,ã DFA co-founder David Booth says to MorôÙris. ãWhat would the world be like if there were no uncerôÙtainôÙty? I mean, pretôÙty dull.ã
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Nobel Prize-WinôÙning PsyôÙcholôÙoôÙgist Daniel KahôÙneôÙman (RIP) Explains the Key QuesôÙtion Every Investor Must Ask, and Why Itãs a Foolãs Errand to Pick Stocks
Errol MorôÙris Makes His GroundôÙbreakôÙing Series First PerôÙson Free to Watch Online: Binge Watch His InterôÙviews with GeniusôÙes, Eccentrics, ObsesôÙsives & OthôÙer UnusuôÙal Types
Take a Free Course on the FinanôÙcial MarôÙkets with Robert Shiller, WinôÙner of the Nobel Prize in EcoôÙnomôÙics
ãThey Were Thereã ã Errol MorôÙris FinalôÙly Directs a Film for IBM
UnderôÙstandôÙing FinanôÙcial MarôÙkets
Watch A Brief HisôÙtoôÙry of Time, Errol MorôÙrisã Film About the Life & Work of Stephen HawkôÙing
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: A Free Course on Karl Marxãs Capital, Volume 1 from Yale University
description: From Yale proôÙfesôÙsor Paul North comes a chapôÙter-by-chapôÙter study of Karl Marxãs CapôÙiôÙtal: CriôÙtique of PolitôÙiôÙcal EconôÙoôÙmy, VolôÙume 1. Acco
body:
From Yale proôÙfesôÙsor Paul North comes a chapôÙter-by-chapôÙter study of Karl Marxãs CapôÙiôÙtal: CriôÙtique of PolitôÙiôÙcal EconôÙoôÙmy, VolôÙume 1. AccordôÙing to the descripôÙtion that accomôÙpaôÙnies the course on YouTube, this ãbook from 1872 is still the best guide to the predaôÙtoôÙry ecoôÙnomôÙic and social sysôÙtem withôÙin which we live. The book solves five basic mysôÙterôÙies in our social world. The mysôÙterôÙies are: why social classôÙes strugôÙgle against one anothôÙer, why human beings are in the thrall of things, how a quanôÙtiôÙty of monôÙey turns into more monôÙey withôÙout seemôÙing to add anyôÙthing, why some peoôÙple are forced to work and the more they work the less they make proôÙporôÙtionôÙal to their effort, and finalôÙly, and why it is so hard to transôÙform the sysôÙtem for the betôÙter.ã You can watch the 19 lecôÙtures from the course in the playlist above.
Prof. North is the co-ediôÙtor of the new EngôÙlish transôÙlaôÙtion and critôÙiôÙcal ediôÙtion of CapôÙiôÙtal VolôÙume 1, and itãs the text used in the course. If youãre interôÙestôÙed in delvôÙing deepôÙer into Marxãs CapôÙiôÙtal, see the David HarôÙvey coursôÙes listôÙed in the RelatôÙeds below.
This course will be added to our list of 1,700 Free Online CoursôÙes from Top UniôÙverôÙsiôÙties

title: Art Retreats in the South of France Offer Mirth, Myth, and Mystery
description: The La Napoule Art Foundation is opening its doors more widely than ever before through its new Threshold Art Retreats, a program designed for art
body:
The La Napoule Art Foundation is opening its doors more widely than ever before through its new Threshold Art Retreats, a program designed for artists seeking creative exploration in an extraordinary setting at the ChûÂteau de La Napoule in the south of France. These immersive five-day retreats invite participants of all backgroundsãnot just professional artistsãto step into a world where art, nature, and personal reflection intersect. With a focus on both artistic practice and inner renewal, the experience offers a rare opportunity to engage deeply with creativity in an ethereal setting shaped by nearly a century of artistic vision.
Each retreat blends hands-on artistic instruction with restorative wellness. Guests might spend their mornings painting in vibrant gardens or working in seaside studios and their afternoons paddleboarding along the Mediterranean or participating in rooftop yoga sessions. Evenings are intentionally reflective, featuring communal meals prepared by the chûÂteauãs chef and meditative experiences like sound bowl sessions in the intimate Loversã Tower. The rhythm of each day is designed to inspire both creative output and personal clarity.
ChûÂteau de La Napoule. Photo by Pip Clews
What makes these retreats especially meaningful is access to mentors. Many are led by accomplished artists who were once residents at the chûÂteau themselves, continuing a powerful cycle of artistic exchange. This structure fosters not just skill-building but also a sense of belonging within a creative lineage. Participants are not simply visitors; they become part of an ongoing artistic community rooted in shared inspiration and dialogue.
Importantly, every retreat directly supports the foundationãs mission. By opening access to a broader audience, the program helps sustain the chûÂteau and its longstanding artist residency programs. It offers a thoughtful solution to a persistent challenge in the art world: how to make transformative, soul-enriching experiences more accessible while maintaining the historic spaces that make them possible. In this way, participation becomes both personally enriching and purpose-driven.
Artist Vanessa Enriquez. Photo by Afton Love
The setting is also inseparable from the experience. Perched above the Mediterranean, the ChûÂteau de La Napoule feels like a living artwork. Its sculptural windows and doors filter the regionãs luminous light, while hybrid figuresãpart human, part imaginedãadorn its columns and courtyards that once hosted Europeãs elite. Acres of whimsical gardens enchant, and the Mediterranean sparkles just beyond the ancient ramparts. A sacred world, perched over the sea, entirely devoted to the arts.
This extraordinary place was brought to life by Henry Clews Jr. and Marie Clews, who, nearly 100 years ago, transformed the ruins of an 11th-century fortress into an artistic sanctuary. Henry used the chûÂteau as his canvas, creating sculptures that critiqued ego and vanity with mythic intensity, while Marie designed its ornamental ironwork and geometric gardens. After Henryãs early death, Marie carried their shared vision forward, founding the La Napoule Art Foundation in 1950. Their mottoããMirth, Myth, Mysteryããremains etched into the stone, a lasting invitation for others to step inside and be transformed.
The ChûÂteau de La Napoule has always been a place where boundaries dissolve, and lives are rewritten. From Henryãs artistic revolution and Marieãs preservation efforts, to countless artists finding new directions there, inspirational change seems to be written into its very walls.ô 
Now, that transformation is available to anyone curious enough to seek it.ô 
Artist Anne-Laure Caro. Photo by Laurent Barnavon
La Napoule Art Foundation offers multiple artist residency opportunities throughout the year and operates as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization in the U.S. Threshold Art Retreats hosts immersive programs each summer from June through September. These retreats feature a rotating calendar of specialized creative experiences, including Foraged Books: The Art of Place (artist bookmaking and storytelling), The Human Form in Oil Paints (figure painting from a live model), and Color as Matter: Exploring Pigment Through Surface and Form (discover the physical and symbolic power of color).
Learn more about residencies and upcoming retreats at lnaf.org and thresholdartretreats.com.
Artist Will Clift. Photo by Mathilde France
Henry Clews Studio. Photo by Bruce Keys

schema.headline: Art Retreats in the South of France Offer Mirth, Myth, and Mystery

title: Barry Webb Documents a Marvelous, Macro Array of Colorful Slime Molds
description: Blown wildly out of proportion in large format, the slime molds that British photographer Barry Webb captures seem atmospheric and sculptural. Ste
body:
Blown wildly out of proportion in large format, the slime molds that British photographer Barry Webb captures seem atmospheric and sculptural. Stemonitis, for example, looks like dozens of thin pieces of wire with their ends coated in colored wax. But this fungi-like form is one of hundreds of kinds of slime mold, and it typically only reaches a height of about two centimeters at the most. Thanks to Webbãs macro photos, we glimpse a phenomenally beautiful world up-close that is otherwise virtually invisible.
Scientists have documented hundreds of these organisms, which arenãt actually related to plants, fungi, animals, or moldsãdespite the name. They comprise a unique group unto themselves, more closely related to amoebas. And new discoveries are being made all the time. From mottled gray bulbs that look like snow-covered trees to pink, coral-like tendrils, Webb chronicles a huge array of colors and shapes. He also consistently submits images to local and national botanical records so that researchers have access to high-resolution imagery.
Didymium squamulosum
Webbãs image of a species called Lamproderma scintillans, partly engulfed by a water droplet, won the Botanical Britain category of the British Wildlife Photography Awards. Several of his photos are on display in large format in the exhibition Mythos Wald at Gasometer Oberhausen in Germany, which continues through the end of the year. And in the U.K., see Webbãs awarded images in the 2026 International Garden Photographer of the Year exhibition at Cambridge University Botanic Garden. Find more on his Instagram.
Cribraria argillacea
Cribraria aurantiaca
Physarum psittacinum and tiny mites
Lamproderma on top of Trichia flavicoma
Deformed Stemonitis
Pink Arcyria

schema.headline: Barry Webb Documents a Marvelous, Macro Array of Colorful Slime Molds

title: Semiprecious Stones Coat Kathleen Ryan's Oversized Sculptures of Rotting Food
description: There are thousands upon thousands of types of mold out there. Some you can eatãthink the rind on a wheel of brie or a gray fungus known as ãnoble
body:
There are thousands upon thousands of types of mold out there. Some you can eatãthink the rind on a wheel of brie or a gray fungus known as ãnoble rotã that gives certain types of grapes an extra sweet flavor for dessert wines. But there are plenty we shouldnãt eat, and when that loaf of bread in the cupboard begins to turn blue-green, itãs definitely time to chuck it in the bin. For Kathleen Ryan, the myriad colors and textures of mold continue to inspire larger-than-life sculptures of fruit and other foods that, in a way, preserve decay.
Ryanãs oversized works are characterized by their textural finishes, often using salvaged metal and other materials in addition to an array of colored beads and semiprecious stones to achieve the effects of layered fungi and rot. Recent works such as ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã and ãScrewdriverã nod to the realm of cocktails and, by extension, the notion of luxury and even vacationsãconcepts that somewhat sour within the context of an increasingly vulnerable economy.
ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã (2024), serpentine, prehnite, azurite in malachite, amazonite, aquamarine, jasper, dolomite, and fuchsite, pyrite, turquoise, labradorite, agate, marble, steel pins on coated polystyrene, Volkswagen fender, 14 x 26 1/2 x 11 1/2 inches
Juxtaposing stones ranging from amethyst and azurite to turquoise and tourmaline with salvaged metal from vintage cars, Ryanãs sculptures evoke an array of associations. She has previously likened their over-the-top scale to the roadside attractions tourists might see along Interstate highways, such as giant doughnuts and other foods and animals.
Like a geode that doesnãt look like much from the outside, works like ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã have two very different personalities, where the metal exterior lets on little about whatãs inside. Ryan taps into our appreciation of hidden beauty when opening up an ancient, crystallized stone while simultaneously suggesting the grotesqueness of opening a peach, for example, only to find it rotten inside.
Some of the works seen here were recently on view at Karma in New York, and you can find more on Ryanãs Instagram.
